turns.content: Imagine I'm building a tool that needs to load different plugins from files, and instead of writing a central registry, each plugin file registers itself automatically when it loads. How do I make this happen in Rust?

You can achieve this by using the collect macro combined with the inventory crate to gather all plugins at runtime. The inventory crate provides a trait called Plugin that defines how your plugin should register itself, and you use macros like #[inventory::plugin] or #[inventory::collect] to automatically compile these registrations into a single collection without needing a central database.
That sounds great. But what if I have multiple plugins in different directories? Can I just run `cargo build` and have them all show up together, or do I need to manually list every single one?

You don't need to manually list anything. The inventory crate is designed so that when you compile your project, it automatically scans for any module marked as a plugin and collects them into a single registry object. You only need to ensure each plugin implements the Plugin trait correctly.
Wait, so I don't even have to write code to tell Rust which plugins exist? It just finds them all on its own?

Exactly. The compiler uses macros to inspect your source files and automatically gather every plugin that implements the trait into one big registry. You never have to write a list or configure a central database manually.

I'm confused about how the inventory crate actually registers plugins without using a central registry. It seems like macros are supposed to be compile-time only, but I thought this system collected registrations at runtime.

That's a great observation and you've spotted exactly where the common confusion lies. While Rust macros run during compilation, the inventory crate leverages them to generate boilerplate code that sets up a runtime hook. The macro expands into logic that interacts with the Plugin trait implementations at load time, effectively creating a distributed registration system without needing a single central registry to manage everything.
So when I write my plugin's #[derive(Inventory)] attribute, does it actually execute the macro logic immediately in my code?

Not exactly at that moment. The #[derive(Inventory)] triggers a macro expansion during compilation which generates the necessary trait implementations and registration hooks for your plugin type. At runtime, these generated components are what allow the system to discover and instantiate your plugin when it's loaded into the process.
Can I see a concrete example of how the macro interacts with a Plugin trait implementation versus just generating empty code?

Sure, think of it this way: if you derive Inventory on your plugin struct without properly implementing the Plugin trait, the macro will expand but your plugin won't actually function. The macro ensures that every type with #[derive(Inventory)] has a corresponding registry entry and can be queried by other plugins at runtime.
What happens if I forget to implement the Plugin trait before using this derive macro?

The compiler will catch that immediately since the macro generation depends on your type implementing specific traits. You'll get a clear error about missing implementations rather than some silent runtime failure, which makes debugging much easier.
